We hired our first head of sales six months too early. The cost wasn't the salary — it was the 14 months of pipeline strategy we built around someone who couldn't yet exist.

Here's what actually happened.

We were 8 people, $400K ARR, growing 12% MoM on inbound from one founder's Twitter. The board pattern-matched: "you're at the stage where you need a sales leader." We agreed. We ran a 4-month search, hired a great VP from a $20M-ARR company, gave her a $180K base and 0.8% equity.

She lasted seven months. Not because she was bad — she was excellent at the job she'd done before. The job we actually needed was "build a repeatable motion from one weird founder-led pipeline." The job we hired for was "scale an existing playbook." Different jobs, same title.

She built the team she knew how to build — two AEs, an SDR, an ops contractor — before we had a documented motion to teach them. Six months and $400K of burn later, we had four people closing on instinct and a CRM full of stages no one trusted. Meanwhile the founder (me) stopped doing the calls that had been our actual sales engine — not because anyone said so, but because the org chart now said someone else owned it. Pipeline cratered. We blamed the market.

The reframe that fixed it: we didn't need a VP of Sales. We needed a Founding AE. Same person working alongside the founder, learning the motion, documenting it as they went, building the playbook BEFORE building the team. We rehired into that role, paid less, gave more equity, and 11 months later had a real motion to scale.

The lesson isn't "don't hire sales early." It's that titles you copy from a later-stage company describe roles that don't exist in yours yet. Hire the title, you get a person optimized for a job you can't give them.

Under $1M ARR and considering a VP of Sales: ask whether you have a motion to scale or a motion to discover. If it's the latter, hire a Founding AE instead. The VP can come later — or never.

#sales #founders #hiring
